content.text: Architecture Exception and Waiver Process

An architecture exception is a formal acknowledgment that an integration does not currently meet a specific gate criterion or architectural standard, combined with a documented plan to resolve the gap within a defined timeframe. Exceptions are not approvals to ignore standards permanently — they are time-limited permits to operate outside the standard while remediation is in progress.

The exception process exists because perfect adherence to all gate criteria at all times is not always achievable. A penetration test firm may have a 6-week lead time that cannot be shortened; a dependency on a third-party security control may not be satisfied until the vendor releases a new version; an integration may be needed for a time-bound event before the full gate process can complete. The exception process addresses these situations without creating a mechanism for systematically bypassing governance.

attrs.alt: Exception and waiver decision flow: assess if non-waivable (BLOCKED), submit exception request, arch lead reviews, grant waiver with conditions and deadline or deny

content.text: What Can and Cannot Be Excepted

The exception process is not available for all gate criteria. Some criteria exist because the risk of non-compliance is too high to allow an exception; others can accommodate a time-limited exception with compensating controls.
Non-Waivable Criteria
The following criteria are not available for exception under any circumstances. An integration that cannot meet these criteria must remediate before proceeding:
DRB decision: APPROVED (Gate 1):

The DRB exists to review architectures before they are built. An integration that is built before DRB approval has bypassed the primary architecture governance mechanism. There is no exception path — the DRB review must occur before build.
Critical and high-severity security findings (Gate 2):

A critical finding is a vulnerability with a realistic path to exploitation in the production environment. Deploying with a critical finding outstanding is not a governance matter — it is a security incident waiting to occur.
Absence of a tested rollback procedure (Gate 3):
An untested rollback means the team does not know whether they can recover from a production failure. This is not a documentation gap — it is an operational safety gap.
Data handling violations:

Where an integration is processing data in a manner inconsistent with applicable regulatory requirements, there is no exception. Regulatory non-compliance is not a matter for the architecture exception process — it is a legal and compliance matter that must be resolved before any deployment.
Waivable Criteria
The following criteria can be excepted with appropriate justification, compensating controls, and a resolution deadline:
Criterion
Exception Conditions
Maximum Exception Duration
Penetration test completed (Gate 2)
Pen test scheduled but not yet complete; integration is in internal-only staging with no external user access
45 days from deployment to staging; pen test must complete before production deployment
ADR review date current (Gate 1)
ADR was accepted within the last 90 days; no material changes to the decision since acceptance
30 days; 90-day review must complete within this window
Runbook tested end-to-end (Gate 3)
Ops team has partial runbook coverage; critical procedures are tested; non-critical procedures are documented but untested
30 days post-deployment; full runbook test must complete within this window
Monitoring dashboard live (Gate 3)
Core metrics are logged; dashboard is configured but not yet deployed; alert routing is not yet tested
14 days post-deployment; monitoring must be fully live
Operations team trained (Gate 3)
Ops lead has completed training; additional team members training is scheduled
30 days post-deployment; all required ops team members must complete training
The Exception Request

An exception request is a short, structured document submitted to the architecture lead. It must be submitted before the team proceeds past the gate — retroactive exception requests (filed after the integration is already in production) are not exception requests; they are incident reports.

ARCHITECTURE EXCEPTION REQUEST
Integration: [name] | Gate: [0 / 1 / 2 / 3]
Requestor: [name, role] | Submission date: YYYY-MM-DD

1. CRITERION NOT MET
[State the specific gate criterion exactly as written. Do not paraphrase.]
Example: "Gate 2: Penetration test completed — All critical and high-severity
findings are resolved."

2. CURRENT STATUS
[Describe the actual state of the criterion today.]
Example: "Penetration test is scheduled with [firm] for 2024-11-20. The test
was originally scheduled for 2024-10-30 but was rescheduled due to [firm]
availability. No test has been completed yet."

3. WHY AN EXCEPTION IS REQUESTED
[Explain the business reason for proceeding without meeting the criterion.
Be specific about the cost of delay.]
Example: "The integration must be available for the annual contract renewal
cycle beginning 2024-11-15. A delay to 2024-11-20 would miss the first week
of the renewal cycle, affecting 40 contracts scheduled for review."

4. COMPENSATING CONTROLS
[Describe the controls in place that reduce risk during the exception period.
An exception without compensating controls is a risk acceptance, not a managed
exception.]
Example: "During the exception period (2024-11-15 to 2024-11-25):
  - Integration will be accessible only to 5 named contract managers (not
    the full user population of 150)
  - System prompt includes an explicit scope restriction that excludes
    sensitive contract categories (M&A, litigation)
  - Operations team will monitor usage logs daily for anomalous patterns"

5. RESOLUTION PLAN AND DEADLINE
[State exactly what will be done and by when to meet the criterion.]
Example: "Penetration test will complete by 2024-11-20. Findings will be
triaged by 2024-11-22. Critical/high findings will be remediated by 2024-11-25.
Full user rollout will not occur until all critical/high findings are resolved."

6. REQUESTOR ATTESTATION
I confirm that the information above is accurate and that the integration
will not exceed the scope described in section 4 during the exception period.
Signature: _______________ Date: ___________

7. EXCEPTION AUTHORITY DECISION
[ ] GRANTED — conditions and deadline as stated above
[ ] GRANTED WITH ADDITIONAL CONDITIONS: [conditions]
[ ] DENIED — reason: [reason]
Architecture lead signature: _______________ Date: ___________
Exception Authority Levels
The authority to grant exceptions is tiered by the risk level of the criterion being excepted. Not all exception decisions rest with the architecture lead.
Exception Type
Approving Authority
Rationale
Standard criterion, duration < 30 days
Architecture lead
Low risk, short duration, routine governance decision
Standard criterion, duration 30–90 days
Architecture lead + Operations lead
Extended exposure requires concurrence from operations perspective
Any criterion involving regulated data categories
Architecture lead + Security lead + Legal/Compliance
Regulatory risk requires specialist sign-off
Any criterion excepted for > 90 days
CTO or equivalent
Extended exceptions indicate a systemic problem; executive visibility required
Any non-waivable criterion
Not available
No authority to grant exception; criterion must be met
The Exception Register

All granted exceptions are recorded in the exception register — a centralised list of all active and closed exceptions across the Claude integration portfolio. The register is the primary tool for tracking whether exceptions are being resolved within their deadlines.

# Exception Register — Claude Integration Programme
# Maintained by: Architecture lead
# Reviewed: Every bi-weekly DRB session

| ID     | Integration    | Gate | Criterion                    | Granted    | Deadline   | Status  | Owner        |
|--------|----------------|------|------------------------------|------------|------------|---------|--------------|
| EXC-001| Contract Review| G2   | Pen test completed           | 2024-11-14 | 2024-11-25 | OPEN    | J. Chen      |
| EXC-002| HR Assist      | G3   | Runbook tested end-to-end    | 2024-10-01 | 2024-10-31 | CLOSED  | M. Okonkwo   |
| EXC-003| Procurement AI | G1   | ADR review date current      | 2024-11-01 | 2024-12-01 | OPEN    | S. Patel     |

STATUS KEY:
  OPEN: exception is within its deadline; criterion not yet met
  AT-RISK: exception is within 7 days of deadline; criterion not yet met
  OVERDUE: deadline has passed; criterion not met; escalated
  CLOSED: criterion met; exception no longer active
Exception Register Review Cadence
The exception register is reviewed at every bi-weekly DRB session. The review takes 5–10 minutes and confirms:
Which exceptions are within 7 days of their deadline (AT-RISK status): the architecture lead contacts the owner to confirm the resolution plan is on track
Which exceptions have passed their deadline (OVERDUE status): these are escalated immediately — the integration is suspended from production or the exception authority level is escalated as required
Which exceptions have been resolved (CLOSED): the gate criterion is re-assessed and the gate passage record is updated
Exception Expiry and Escalation
An exception that is not resolved by its deadline does not automatically renew. An overdue exception triggers an escalation to the next authority level:
Exception becomes OVERDUE on deadline date
Architecture lead contacts integration owner within 24 hours to determine why the criterion has not been met
If resolution is imminent (< 7 days): the exception may be extended by the original approving authority for a maximum of 14 additional days, once only
If resolution is not imminent: the exception is escalated to the next authority level (e.g., CTO for exceptions originally approved by architecture lead + operations lead)
If the escalating authority determines the risk is unacceptable: the integration is suspended from production use until the criterion is met

An overdue exception that is silently extended by the operations team, without going through the escalation process, is not an exception — it is a compliance violation. The escalation process is not punitive; it is a signal that the exception is more complex than anticipated and requires higher-level attention. Teams that are transparent about exception status get help resolving them; teams that quietly extend exceptions get surprised by audit findings.
Waivers vs. Standard Amendments

A waiver is distinct from a standard amendment to an architectural standard. A waiver is granted for a specific integration for a specific period — it is not a change to the standard itself. If a gate criterion is regularly waived (three or more exceptions across the portfolio in a 6-month period), that is evidence that the criterion itself may need revision.
When the exception register shows a pattern of repeated exceptions against the same criterion:
The architecture lead proposes a formal review of the criterion at the next DRB session
The review assesses whether the criterion is calibrated correctly for the portfolio (too strict, not strict enough, or appropriate and the exceptions indicate a delivery problem)
If the criterion is revised, the gate documentation is updated and existing exceptions are re-assessed against the revised criterion
If the criterion is confirmed correct, the delivery teams are coached on meeting the criterion earlier in the development cycle to avoid exceptions
Claude-Specific Exception Scenarios

Claude integrations encounter exception scenarios that do not arise in traditional software projects. The following are common patterns, along with the compensating controls and resolution paths that have proven effective.
Scenario 1: Model Evaluation Not Complete Before Production Deadline

A team has selected a model tier but has not completed the formal evaluation (eval suite) against their production use case before the deployment deadline. The business requires the integration to be live for a specific event.
Exception justification:
Formal eval suite development requires representative production data, which will only be available after initial deployment to a limited user population.
Compensating controls:

Deploy to a named user group only (not the full population); require operations team to review every response during the first 2 weeks manually; set a hard limit of 50 requests per day to bound the exposure.
Resolution plan:
Collect 200 representative request/response pairs from the limited deployment. Build eval suite against these cases. Run eval. Expand deployment only after eval passes the quality threshold.
Deadline:
Eval suite built and passing within 6 weeks of initial deployment; full user rollout contingent on passing.
Scenario 2: System Prompt Governance Policy Not Yet Approved

The integration is ready for production but the organisation has not yet finalised its system prompt governance policy (the cross-functional policy governing who can change system prompts and what approval is required). The team cannot wait for the policy to be approved.
Exception justification:
Policy development is in progress at programme level; the delay is outside the team's control.
Compensating controls:

The team implements a temporary local policy: all system prompt changes require approval from both the integration lead and the operations lead; changes are logged with a timestamp and the name of both approvers; no change is deployed without a 24-hour review period.
Resolution plan:
The programme-level policy is expected to be approved within 60 days. When approved, the local policy is replaced with the programme policy without a gap.
Deadline:
60 days from integration go-live.
Scenario 3: Knowledge Base Owner Not Identified

The integration uses a retrieval-augmented generation architecture, and the knowledge base includes content from multiple business units. A named owner for the knowledge base content has not been identified before go-live.
Exception justification:
The BU content owners are in a reorganisation; named ownership will be confirmed within 30 days.
Compensating controls:

The PoC team assumes temporary custodianship of the knowledge base during the exception period. No content updates are made during this period without written approval from the BU sponsor. The knowledge base is frozen at its current content.
Resolution plan:
Named owners confirmed and documented in the runbook within 30 days. Each content area has a named owner and a backup.
Deadline:
30 days from go-live.
Scenario 4: API Tier Upgrade Pending
The integration's production load requires a higher API tier than the team currently has access to. The tier upgrade has been requested but Anthropic's provisioning process takes 5–10 business days.
Exception justification:
Tier upgrade request submitted; provisioning is outside the team's control.
Compensating controls:

Deploy to a limited user population whose aggregate request volume is within the current tier limits. Implement a request queue with rate limiting that caps the request rate below the current tier's limits. Monitor the rate limit headroom daily.
Resolution plan:
Tier upgrade confirmed and active before expanding the user population beyond the limited group.
Deadline:
15 business days from integration go-live.
Documenting the Business Impact of Exceptions

An exception request that cannot articulate the business cost of delay is a weak request. The architecture lead needs to understand the tradeoff: the risk of proceeding without meeting the criterion against the business cost of not proceeding. A well-documented business impact makes that tradeoff explicit.

BUSINESS IMPACT SECTION (for exception requests where deadline pressure is cited)

Integration: Contract Review Assistant
Gate: 2 (Security)
Criterion: Penetration test completed
Business deadline: 2024-11-15 (annual contract renewal cycle begins)

IMPACT OF PROCEEDING WITHOUT EXCEPTION:
  If the integration is not available from 2024-11-15:
  - 40 contracts due for renewal in the first week will be reviewed manually
    (current process: 12.4 days average cycle time)
  - At current FTE cost, manual review of 40 contracts requires approximately
    240 attorney-hours, compared to 85 attorney-hours with the integration
  - Estimated incremental cost: $31,500 (155 hours × $203/hour blended rate)
  - 3 contracts are flagged as high-priority; delay in these renewals has
    an estimated risk of $800k in contract value at risk

RISK OF PROCEEDING WITH EXCEPTION:
  The integration will be in production without a completed pen test for
  10 days (2024-11-15 to 2024-11-25).
  During this period:
  - Only contract managers handle data (no external parties)
  - Data classification: internal business documents, no personal data
  - Attack surface is limited: integration accessible only via internal
    network with corporate SSO
  - Compensating controls as described in section 4

REQUESTOR'S RISK ASSESSMENT:
  The business cost of delay ($31,500 + contract risk) exceeds the
  security risk of a 10-day exception with compensating controls,
  in the requestor's judgment. This assessment is provided for the
  architecture lead's consideration.
Exception Anti-Patterns
The following practices undermine the value of the exception process and should be identified and corrected when observed:
Anti-Pattern
How to Identify It
Corrective Action
Exception as default path
A team routinely submits exceptions rather than meeting criteria on schedule
Review the team's delivery process; exceptions should be < 20% of gate passages; chronic exceptions indicate a resourcing or timeline problem that requires intervention, not more exceptions
Compensating controls that are not controls
Compensating control says 'we will monitor closely' with no definition of what is being monitored, at what frequency, or what threshold triggers action
Exception is returned for revision; compensating controls must be specific (metric, frequency, threshold, action)
Retroactive exception requests
Team deploys to production, then files an exception request for a criterion they did not meet
Retroactive requests are not accepted; the integration is suspended until the gate criterion is met or an exception is prospectively approved
Exception stacking
A single integration has 4+ simultaneous active exceptions
CTO-level review required; an integration with 4+ simultaneous exceptions has a governance problem, not an exception problem
Resolution deadline optimism
Exception deadlines are consistently missed; same exceptions appear as OVERDUE repeatedly
Architecture lead escalates to operations lead; if pattern continues, integration is suspended from production until criteria are met without exception

The exception process is a safety valve, not a standard delivery option. When a programme finds that more than 20% of gate transitions involve an exception, the exception process is masking a delivery problem that needs direct intervention: inadequate timelines, under-resourced teams, criteria that are too aspirational for the programme's maturity level, or a cultural acceptance of governance shortcuts. Addressing the root cause is always more effective than processing more exceptions.
Summary

The architecture exception and waiver process provides a controlled mechanism for integrations to proceed when specific gate criteria cannot be met on schedule. The process is not an escape hatch from governance — it is a structured way to manage the gap between the ideal (all criteria met on time) and the practical (dependencies, lead times, and delivery constraints that make perfect adherence occasionally impossible).

The non-waivable list protects the standards that must not be traded away: DRB review before build, critical security findings resolved before production, and regulatory compliance at all times. The exception register and bi-weekly review ensure that granted exceptions are tracked and resolved, not forgotten. The escalation process ensures that overdue exceptions receive attention at the authority level appropriate to the risk. Together, these elements make the exception process a tool for maintaining governance quality over time, not eroding it.
